title: Women's Worth: Classical Christian Voices and the Islamic Tradition
subtitle: What Tertullian, Jerome, Aquinas, and Basil wrote about women — verified and in context — read alongside the Quran, the hadith, and both traditions' modern responses
summary: A documentary survey of what four influential classical Christian authorities — Tertullian, Jerome, Thomas Aquinas, and Basil of Caesarea — wrote about women, with every quotation verified against standard scholarly editions and cited by work and section. The same standard of honesty is applied to the Islamic tradition, including the contested "deficient in intellect and religion" hadith and the Quran's equality verses. Both traditions' mainstream responses and internal reform conversations are presented at full strength, with no winner declared.

seo.metaTitle: Women's Worth: Classical Christian Voices and the Islamic Tradition
seo.metaDescription: Verified quotations from Tertullian, Jerome, Aquinas, and Basil on women, placed beside the Quran and hadith — with both traditions' modern responses and reform debates, fairly presented.
keywords: deficient in intellect and religion hadith

sections.title: What This Survey Does
sections.body:
This article is a documentary survey. It gathers what four of the most influential classical Christian authorities — Tertullian, Jerome, Basil of Caesarea, and Thomas Aquinas — actually wrote about women, quoting each from a standard scholarly translation and naming the work and section so any reader can check the words. It then places those texts beside the Islamic tradition's own record: the hadith most often raised against Islam on this subject, quoted in full with its precise citation, and the Quranic passages that Muslims cite as the tradition's charter of spiritual equality.

The rules of the survey are strict. Every quotation was verified character-for-character against a named, publicly accessible edition: the Ante-Nicene Fathers and Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers translations hosted at newadvent.org and tertullian.org, the English Dominican translation of the Summa Theologiae, the World English Bible, the Tanzil Uthmani text with the Saheeh International translation, and the standard English hadith translations following sunnah.com numbering. One famous line commonly attributed to Jerome could not be verified in an accessible primary text; it is reported below as an attribution, with that caveat stated plainly, rather than quoted as verified.

What the survey is not: it is not evidence that either faith, as a faith, despises women, and it is not a scorecard. Both traditions contain harsh texts, generous texts, and long centuries of interpreters arguing over both kinds. The later sections present the mainstream Christian responses to these patristic and medieval passages at full strength, apply the same honesty to the Islamic corpus, and end with the reform conversations happening inside each tradition. The article deliberately declares no winner.

sections.title: The Texts Both Traditions Start From
sections.body:
Classical authorities did not write in a vacuum; they interpreted scriptures their communities already held sacred, through the science and custom of their times. On the Christian side, the baseline texts pull in more than one direction. Genesis 1:27 places male and female together in the creation of God's image: "God created man in his own image. In God's image he created him; male and female he created them." Genesis 2:18 introduces the word that Aquinas would later build on: "It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make him a helper comparable to him." The Levitical purity code, including Leviticus 15:19 on menstrual impurity, shaped later Christian debates about women and sacred space even though most Christians held the ceremonial law fulfilled. In the New Testament, 1 Timothy 2:12-14 grounds a teaching restriction in Eve's creation order and deception, while Galatians 3:28 declares that in Christ "there is neither male nor female." Which of these texts controls the reading of the others is precisely what Christians have argued about for two millennia.

The Islamic baseline shows a parallel structure. Quran 4:1 addresses humanity as created "from one soul" from which its mate was created — with no doctrine of Eve as the first sinner; in the Quranic account of the garden, Adam and his wife err together and are forgiven together. Quran 33:35 lists ten spiritual virtues in deliberately paired masculine and feminine forms and promises both sexes the same forgiveness and reward. Quran 9:71 makes believing men and women awliya — allies and protectors — of one another. Alongside these stand texts Muslims debate internally, notably Quran 2:228, which affirms that wives' rights mirror their obligations "according to what is reasonable" while adding that men have "a degree" over them — a phrase whose meaning (responsibility for maintenance, divorce procedure, or broader authority) is itself a centuries-old interpretive dispute.

With those baselines in view, the classical voices can be heard as what they were: interpreters, not founders.

sections.title: Tertullian: "You Are the Devil's Gateway"
sections.body:
Tertullian of Carthage (c. 155-220) stands at the head of Latin Christianity. He is often called the father of Western theology, and he is generally credited as the first Latin author whose surviving works apply the word trinitas to God — the term that became the Latin church's name for its central doctrine. His treatise On the Apparel of Women (De cultu feminarum) is a two-book exhortation urging Christian women toward plain, penitential dress. Its opening chapter — addressed, in his own words, to his "best beloved sisters" — contains perhaps the most quoted sentence any church father ever wrote about women:

"And do you not know that you are (each) an Eve? The sentence of God on this sex of yours lives in this age: the guilt must of necessity live too. You are the devil's gateway: you are the unsealer of that (forbidden) tree: you are the first deserter of the divine law: you are she who persuaded him whom the devil was not valiant enough to attack. You destroyed so easily God's image, man. On account of your desert — that is, death — even the Son of God had to die." (On the Apparel of Women, Book I, chapter 1; Ante-Nicene Fathers translation, newadvent.org)

The argument is explicit: Eve's guilt lives on in every woman, and a Christian woman should therefore dress as one mourning and repentant. The reasoning extends the logic found in 1 Timothy 2:12-14 — where Eve's deception grounds a restriction on women teaching — into a claim about inherited, sex-specific guilt that goes beyond anything the New Testament text itself states.

Readers who defend Tertullian's memory add context rather than denial. The treatise belongs to the flamboyant rhetoric of Roman North African moralism; its addressees are women he calls beloved sisters and treats as full members of the church whose conduct can glorify God; and in his treatise To His Wife the same author describes the shared prayer and charity of a Christian couple in warm, admiring terms. Historians also note the irony that Tertullian later attached himself to the New Prophecy movement, which honored the prophetesses Prisca and Maximilla. None of that erases the passage; it locates it. Tertullian was never canonized, and no church treats his sentences as doctrine.

sections.title: Jerome: Asceticism, Marriage, and the Purity Question
sections.body:
Jerome (c. 342-420), translator of the Latin Vulgate, was the most formidable biblical scholar of the early Latin church, and his letters and polemics carried immense weight in the medieval West. Two strands of his writing matter here.

The first is his ascetic ranking of women's states of life. In Against Jovinianus (393), answering a monk who taught that marriage and virginity stood equal before God, Jerome pressed Paul's words in 1 Corinthians 7:1 — "it is good for a man not to touch a woman" — to a severe conclusion: "It is good, he says, for a man not to touch a woman. If it is good not to touch a woman, it is bad to touch one: for there is no opposite to goodness but badness." (Against Jovinianus, Book I, section 7; Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers translation, newadvent.org). In the same book he ranks virginity, widowhood, and marriage as hundredfold, sixtyfold, and thirtyfold fruit. His Letter 22 to Eustochium states the hierarchy with famous bluntness: "I praise wedlock, I praise marriage, but it is because they give me virgins. I gather the rose from the thorns, the gold from the earth, the pearl from the shell." (Letter 22, section 20). Contemporaries protested that this effectively disparaged marriage — and Jerome spent years insisting he had not condemned it.

The second strand is purity. A line widely repeated in histories of menstruation — that nothing is so unclean as a menstruating woman, and that what she touches becomes unclean — is commonly attributed to Jerome's commentary on Zechariah. This survey could not verify that wording in an accessible primary text, so it is reported here strictly as an attribution, not as a verified quotation. What is documentable is the framework around it. Leviticus 15:19 declares a menstruating woman ritually impure, and parts of the early church debated whether such purity rules touched Christian worship. Dionysius of Alexandria's canonical letter to Basilides (c. 247) ruled: "Menstruous women ought not to come to the Holy Table, or touch the Holy of Holies, nor to churches, but pray elsewhere." (Canonical Epistle, Canon II; Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, second series, vol. 14, tertullian.org). Yet the tradition was not uniform: the responsum of Pope Gregory I preserved in Bede's Ecclesiastical History (Book I, chapter 27) ruled the other way, holding that a menstruating woman should not be forbidden church or communion, while praising restraint as devout. Historians of Christian worship, such as Elizabeth Clark, document how purity-based hesitations nonetheless contributed in some times and places to distancing women from the altar and from liturgical roles.

sections.title: Aquinas: "Defective and Misbegotten" and the Biology Behind It
sections.body:
Thomas Aquinas (c. 1225-1274) is the most authoritative theologian of the medieval Latin church, and his Summa Theologiae remains a touchstone of Catholic thought. In the First Part, question 92, article 1, he asks whether woman should have been made in the first production of things. The first objection quotes Aristotle's biology: "the female is a misbegotten male." Aquinas answers that woman's creation was necessary — but his stated reason narrows her purpose: "It was necessary for woman to be made, as the Scripture says, as a 'helper' to man; not, indeed, as a helpmate in other works, as some say, since man can be more efficiently helped by another man in other works; but as a helper in the work of generation." (Summa Theologiae I, q. 92, a. 1; English Dominican translation, newadvent.org). This reads Genesis 2:18's "helper" as a specifically reproductive office.

His reply to the Aristotelian objection concedes its biology at the individual level: "As regards the individual nature, woman is defective and misbegotten, for the active force in the male seed tends to the production of a perfect likeness in the masculine sex; while the production of woman comes from defect in the active force or from some material indisposition, or even from some external influence; such as that of a south wind, which is moist, as the Philosopher observes" — before adding the correction that "as regards human nature in general, woman is not misbegotten, but is included in nature's intention as directed to the work of generation." In the Latin, the operative words are deficiens and occasionatum. The framework is Aristotle's Generation of Animals: the male seed was thought to be the active, form-giving principle aiming to reproduce its like, so that a female birth signaled the seed's aim incompletely realized. Aquinas also holds, in the same question, that "woman is naturally subject to man, because in man the discretion of reason predominates" — a subjection he classes as the benign, "economic or civil" kind that would have existed even before sin. In question 93 he affirms that "the image of God, in its principal signification, namely the intellectual nature, is found both in man and in woman," while adding that "in a secondary sense the image of God is found in man, and not in woman."

Modern Christian assessment spans a spectrum. The egalitarian organization CBE International published a pointed critique by church historian Joe Early ("Thomas Aquinas's 'Misbegotten' Concept of Women," Priscilla Papers 38.4, 2024), arguing that Aquinas's fusion of Aristotelian embryology with theology cast women as defective males suited mainly for childbearing, that this diminished their share in God's image in practice, and that the prestige his system enjoyed after the Council of Trent carried the obsolete biology deep into later tradition. Defenders of Aquinas reply that he explicitly denies woman is a defect in nature's overall intention, that both sexes for him are directly willed by God and equally bear the image of God in its principal sense, and that the "misbegotten" language collapses with the medieval science it depended on — which the Catholic Church has long since abandoned.

sections.title: Basil of Caesarea: Canon 9 and the Double Standard of Custom
sections.body:
Basil of Caesarea (c. 330-379), one of the three Cappadocian fathers and a saint in both Eastern and Western Christianity, wrote a series of canonical letters to Amphilochius, bishop of Iconium, that were later received into Eastern Orthodox canon law. Letter 188, his First Canonical Letter (c. 374), addresses in its ninth canon the asymmetry between men and women in divorce.

Basil begins by acknowledging that the Gospel standard is symmetrical: "The sentence of the Lord that it is unlawful to withdraw from wedlock, save on account of fornication, applies, according to the argument, to men and women alike. Custom, however, does not so obtain." (Letter 188, canon 9; Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers translation, newadvent.org). The "sentence of the Lord" is Jesus' teaching in Matthew 5:32. Basil then records the double standard of his society without overturning it: "Yet custom ordains that men who commit adultery and are in fornication be retained by their wives." A wife's grounds for leaving are examined one by one, and it is here that the canon's hardest sentence appears: "In the case of her being beaten, and refusing to submit, it would be better for her to endure than to be separated from her husband; in the case of her objecting to pecuniary loss, even here she would not have sufficient ground."

Read carefully, the canon does two things at once. It candidly notices that custom contradicts the Lord's even-handed rule — Basil twice contrasts what "custom" ordains with what the Gospel says, and some historians read those remarks as implicit criticism of the custom he was bound to administer. And yet it counsels a beaten wife to endure rather than separate, and penalizes her, not her husband, if she leaves. Eastern canonists later worked within this framework for centuries.

Modern churches of every major tradition have explicitly repudiated the pastoral application of such counsel to abuse. The United States Catholic bishops' pastoral statement When I Call for Help (1992, reaffirmed 2002) condemns the use of the Bible to keep women in abusive relationships and states that separation for safety is morally legitimate; comparable statements exist across Orthodox and Protestant bodies. Defenders of Basil emphasize that a fourth-century bishop regulating Roman-era custom is not a lawgiver for the present, and that his own writings elsewhere show deep pastoral care; critics answer that the canon shaped real women's options for a very long time. Both statements are true to the record.

sections.title: The Mainstream Christian Response, at Full Strength
sections.body:
Christian scholars and churches offer a layered response to this dossier, and fairness requires stating it at its strongest.

First, a category distinction: none of these men wrote scripture. Christian doctrine is normed by the biblical canon; the fathers and doctors are honored witnesses, not oracles, and every major tradition holds them corrigible. The church never defined Aristotle's embryology as dogma, and Tertullian's harshest treatise carries no canonical authority in any communion.

Second, the same men are more complicated than their worst sentences. Tertullian addressed the women he rebuked as "best beloved sisters." Jerome's closest intellectual companions were learned women — Marcella, Paula, and Eustochium — whom he taught Hebrew exegesis and praised extravagantly in his letters, and whose scholarship he defended against male critics. Basil explicitly flagged the tension between the Gospel's symmetry and his society's custom. Aquinas insisted that women fully possess the rational soul and the image of God in its principal sense. A dossier of their generous passages could be assembled with equal documentary rigor.

Third, scripture supplies the internal counter-voice. Galatians 3:28 — "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free man, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus" — has anchored Christian egalitarianism from the abolitionists to the present. The Gospels place women among Jesus' disciples and as the first witnesses of the resurrection, in a culture that discounted women's testimony.

Fourth, modern repudiation is explicit and official. Pope John Paul II's 1995 Letter to Women acknowledged the church's historical share in women's marginalization: "And if objective blame, especially in particular historical contexts, has belonged to not just a few members of the Church, for this I am truly sorry." (Letter to Women, n. 3, vatican.va). His 1988 apostolic letter Mulieris Dignitatem grounded women's equal dignity in creation and in Christ. The Catechism of the Catholic Church teaches that man and woman are created in perfect equality as human persons. Protestant egalitarians (organized in bodies such as CBE International) argue that full equality of authority is the biblical position itself, while complementarians — for example, in the 1987 Danvers Statement — affirm equal worth and dignity with differentiated roles and likewise reject the patristic language of female defectiveness. In short, no living Christian tradition defends "defective and misbegotten" as anthropology; the intra-Christian debate today is about roles, not worth.

Finally, honest Christian scholarship concedes the historical cost. Historians within the church, from Elizabeth Clark onward, document that these texts had real institutional effects — in canon law, in purity-based exclusions, in the medieval reading of women's capacities — and hold that acknowledging this history is part of repairing it.

sections.title: The Same Honesty Applied to the Islamic Tradition
sections.body:
A survey built only from Christian texts would be propaganda, not comparison. The Islamic corpus contains its own hard text, and it sits in Sahih al-Bukhari, the collection Sunni Muslims regard as the most rigorously authenticated book after the Quran.

In Sahih al-Bukhari 304 (Book of Menstrual Periods; Book 6, hadith 9), Abu Sa'id al-Khudri narrates that the Prophet, passing the women after a festival prayer, said: "O women! Give alms, as I have seen that the majority of the dwellers of Hell-fire were you (women)." Asked why, he replied: "You curse frequently and are ungrateful to your husbands. I have not seen anyone more deficient in intelligence and religion than you. A cautious sensible man could be led astray by some of you." When the women asked what the deficiency was, he answered: "Is not the evidence of two women equal to the witness of one man?" and "This is the deficiency in her intelligence. Isn't it true that a woman can neither pray nor fast during her menses?" — "This is the deficiency in her religion."

Canonical Sunni hadith scholarship has generally accepted this report's authenticity (Sahih al-Bukhari 304), while some modern Muslim hadith-critical and feminist scholars have challenged its transmission, scope, or interpretation; it has argued about its meaning. The classical commentators — Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani in Fath al-Bari on Bukhari, and al-Nawawi on the parallel report in Sahih Muslim 79a — make several contextualizing moves. The setting is a festival-day exhortation to charity, a genre of pointed pastoral rhetoric rather than a treatise on female nature. The "deficiency in religion" is defined inside the hadith itself: menstruating women are exempted from prayer and fasting — an exemption legislated by God, for which, commentators stress, a woman bears no blame and loses no standing before God; the wording describes a smaller quantity of ritual acts, not a smaller soul. The "intelligence" remark is tied by the text to a specific evidentiary procedure for documenting debts (treated in detail in the companion article on inheritance and testimony), and classical jurists did not generalize it — in domains such as women's own expertise, a single woman's testimony was accepted. Modern Muslim discussion divides: some scholars read the report as occasion-specific rhetoric that later readers wrongly universalized; others regard it as a normative description tied to those two rulings and nothing more; still others, including some Muslim feminists, argue openly about its implications. Presenting the hadith as either a settled insult or a settled compliment would misrepresent a live debate.

The same corpus carries the tradition's counterweights, with equally strong chains of transmission in their collections. In Jami at-Tirmidhi 3895, Aishah narrates: "The best of you is the best to his wives, and I am the best of you to my wives." In Jami at-Tirmidhi 1162: "The most complete of the believers in faith, is the one with the best character among them. And the best of you are those who are best to your women." And the Quran itself supplies the baseline texts quoted earlier: creation from one soul (4:1), mutual guardianship of believing men and women (9:71), and the ten-fold parallel virtues with identical reward (33:35) — alongside 2:228's contested "degree," which Muslims debate much as Christians debate their own hierarchy texts. Note that the bracketed gloss "[in responsibility and authority]" in the Saheeh International rendering of 2:228 is a translator's interpretive addition, not part of the Arabic text — an example of how interpretation enters even at the level of translation.

sections.title: Two Internal Reform Conversations
sections.body:
Both traditions are, right now, arguing with their own archives — and in both cases the argument is conducted from inside, with each side claiming the tradition's deepest sources for itself.

Within Christianity, the spectrum runs from egalitarians, who hold that Galatians 3:28 and the practice of Jesus set the norm and that the patristic and medieval material represents cultural accretion (the case made by historians such as Beth Allison Barr in The Making of Biblical Womanhood), to complementarians, who maintain differentiated roles while repudiating any language of female inferiority. Denominational practice now spans women bishops in the Anglican Communion and women pastors across much of Protestantism, alongside the Catholic and Orthodox retention of a male priesthood justified — in those churches' own words — by sacramental symbolism rather than by any deficiency in women. The Catholic magisterium's twentieth-century reversal of the older anthropology, from Mulieris Dignitatem to the 1995 apology, is itself a documented reform of the tradition surveyed above.

Within Islam, the recovery of women's scholarly history has become a major project. Mohammad Akram Nadwi's al-Muhaddithat, the introduction to his multi-volume biographical dictionary, documents thousands of women hadith scholars across Islamic history — women who taught men, certified transmissions, and issued corrections. The classical scholar al-Zarkashi (d. 1392) devoted an entire work, al-Ijaba, to the instances in which Aishah corrected the Companions' reports — an early, canonical precedent for a woman's scholarly authority over men. Contemporary debates over women's mosque access, female religious authority and iftaa, and family-law reform (Morocco's 2004 family-code revision is a frequently cited legislative example) proceed by contesting interpretations of the same Quranic verses and hadiths quoted in this article, with reformist and traditionalist scholars each claiming fidelity to the sources.

The symmetry is the finding. Each tradition contains foundational texts of equality, historical strata of hierarchy, and living communities deciding how the two relate. Which community is handling that inheritance better is a judgment this article leaves entirely to the reader.

sections.title: Sources and Verification
sections.body:
Every quotation in this article was checked against the following editions, and each is cited in the text by work and section.

Tertullian, On the Apparel of Women, Book I, chapter 1 — Ante-Nicene Fathers, vol. 4, at newadvent.org/fathers/0402.htm. Jerome, Against Jovinianus, Book I, section 7 — Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, second series, vol. 6, at newadvent.org/fathers/30091.htm; Letter 22 to Eustochium, section 20, at newadvent.org/fathers/3001022.htm. The menstruation line attributed to Jerome's commentary on Zechariah could not be verified in an accessible primary text and is therefore reported only as an attribution. Dionysius of Alexandria, Canonical Epistle to Basilides, Canon II — Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, second series, vol. 14, at tertullian.org. Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologiae I, q. 92, a. 1 and q. 93, a. 4 — English Dominican Province translation, at newadvent.org/summa/1092.htm and /summa/1093.htm. Basil of Caesarea, Letter 188, canon 9 — Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, second series, vol. 8, at newadvent.org/fathers/3202188.htm. Pope John Paul II, Letter to Women, n. 3 (1995), at vatican.va. Joe Early, "Thomas Aquinas's 'Misbegotten' Concept of Women," Priscilla Papers 38.4 (2024), CBE International, at cbeinternational.org.

Hadith citations follow sunnah.com numbering: Sahih al-Bukhari 304 (Book of Menstrual Periods; Book 6, hadith 9); Jami at-Tirmidhi 3895 (Chapters on Virtues) and 1162 (Chapters on Suckling), in the standard English translations. Quran quotations use the Tanzil Uthmani text with the Saheeh International translation via api.alquran.cloud; Bible quotations use the World English Bible via bible-api.com. Classical commentary positions (Ibn Hajar's Fath al-Bari, al-Nawawi's commentary on Sahih Muslim, al-Zarkashi's al-Ijaba) are summarized, not quoted, and are attributed by name so readers can pursue them.

sections.title: Notes on Reading This Material Fairly
sections.body:
Three cautions apply to everything above. First, quotation-mining cuts both ways: a survey of any tradition's harshest sentences will distort unless the counter-texts and the historical settings are kept in view, which is why this article quotes the responses of each tradition at full strength and names its sources throughout. Second, the classical authors wrote inside the science and social order of their eras — Aristotelian embryology, Roman family law, late-antique purity culture — and holding them to knowledge they could not have had explains their errors without excusing the harm those errors did. Third, the Victorian-era translations quoted here (Ante-Nicene and Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers) are the standard accessible English versions but are themselves dated; serious students should consult modern critical editions where wording matters.

This material is offered as information for readers comparing the two traditions honestly — not as ammunition. Behind every text in this article are living communities of women and men who love their faith; both traditions' own scholars, quoted above, model how to face a difficult inheritance without contempt.

quranVerses.arabic: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍۢ وَٰحِدَةٍۢ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًۭا كَثِيرًۭا وَنِسَآءًۭ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًۭا
quranVerses.translation: O mankind, fear your Lord, who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women. And fear Allah, through whom you ask one another, and the wombs. Indeed Allah is ever, over you, an Observer.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَٰتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍۢ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَيُطِيعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: The believing men and believing women are allies of one another. They enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and establish prayer and give zakah and obey Allah and His Messenger. Those - Allah will have mercy upon them. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ وَٱلْمُسْلِمَٰتِ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَٰتِ وَٱلْقَٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْقَٰنِتَٰتِ وَٱلصَّٰدِقِينَ وَٱلصَّٰدِقَٰتِ وَٱلصَّٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّٰبِرَٰتِ وَٱلْخَٰشِعِينَ وَٱلْخَٰشِعَٰتِ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقَٰتِ وَٱلصَّٰٓئِمِينَ وَٱلصَّٰٓئِمَٰتِ وَٱلْحَٰفِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَٱلْحَٰفِظَٰتِ وَٱلذَّٰكِرِينَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًۭا وَٱلذَّٰكِرَٰتِ أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةًۭ وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Indeed, the Muslim men and Muslim women, the believing men and believing women, the obedient men and obedient women, the truthful men and truthful women, the patient men and patient women, the humble men and humble women, the charitable men and charitable women, the fasting men and fasting women, the men who guard their private parts and the women who do so, and the men who remember Allah often and the women who do so - for them Allah has prepared forgiveness and a great reward.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَٱلْمُطَلَّقَٰتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ ثَلَٰثَةَ قُرُوٓءٍۢ ۚ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَرْحَامِهِنَّ إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْءَاخِرِ ۚ وَبُعُولَتُهُنَّ أَحَقُّ بِرَدِّهِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ إِنْ أَرَادُوٓا۟ إِصْلَٰحًۭا ۚ وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ وَلِلرِّجَالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجَةٌۭ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ
quranVerses.translation: Divorced women remain in waiting for three periods, and it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs if they believe in Allah and the Last Day. And their husbands have more right to take them back in this [period] if they want reconciliation. And due to the wives is similar to what is expected of them, according to what is reasonable. But the men have a degree over them [in responsibility and authority]. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: God created man in his own image. In God’s image he created him; male and female he created them.
Yahweh God said, “It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make him a helper comparable to him.”
“‘If a woman has a discharge, and her discharge in her flesh is blood, she shall be in her impurity seven days: and whoever touches her shall be unclean until the evening.
but I tell you that whoever puts away his wife, except for the cause of sexual immorality, makes her an adulteress; and whoever marries her when she is put away commits adultery.
Now concerning the things about which you wrote to me: it is good for a man not to touch a woman.
There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free man, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus.

But I don’t permit a woman to teach, nor to exercise authority over a man, but to be in quietness. For Adam was first formed, then Eve. Adam wasn’t deceived, but the woman, being deceived, has fallen into disobedience;

faq.question: Are the shocking quotations from the church fathers genuine?
faq.answer: Yes. Every quotation in this article was verified character-for-character against standard scholarly editions — the Ante-Nicene Fathers and Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers translations at newadvent.org and tertullian.org, and the English Dominican translation of Aquinas's Summa Theologiae — and each is cited by work and section (for example, On the Apparel of Women I.1; Summa Theologiae I q.92 a.1; Letter 188 canon 9). The one exception is a menstruation line commonly attributed to Jerome, which could not be verified in an accessible primary text and is therefore reported only as an attribution.

faq.question: Do these quotations represent official Christian doctrine?
faq.answer: No. Christian doctrine is normed by the biblical canon, and the fathers and medieval doctors — however influential — are regarded by every major tradition as honored but corrigible witnesses. No church ever defined Aristotle's embryology or Tertullian's rhetoric as dogma, and modern Christian bodies across the spectrum explicitly teach the equal dignity of women, from the Catholic Catechism and John Paul II's 1995 apology to Protestant statements on both the egalitarian and complementarian sides.

faq.question: Is the "deficient in intelligence and religion" hadith authentic, and what do Muslim scholars say it means?
faq.answer: The report is in Sahih al-Bukhari (hadith 304), which Sunni Muslims regard as rigorously authenticated, so mainstream scholarship debates its meaning rather than its authenticity. Classical commentators such as Ibn Hajar and al-Nawawi note that the hadith itself defines the two "deficiencies" as specific rulings — an evidentiary procedure for debt contracts and the menstrual exemption from prayer and fasting, an exemption legislated by God for which a woman bears no blame. Modern Muslim discussion is divided between reading the wording as occasion-specific festival rhetoric and reading it as limited normative description; this article presents that debate as contested rather than settled.

faq.question: Why does the article quote harsh texts from both traditions instead of just one?
faq.answer: Because a one-sided dossier is propaganda, not comparison. The site's method is to state each side's case at its honest strongest: the Christian patristic and medieval texts are quoted exactly and answered by the strongest mainstream Christian responses, and the Islamic tradition's most contested hadith is quoted exactly and answered by its own classical and modern contextualizations. Readers can only judge fairly when both archives are opened to the same depth.

faq.question: Did later Christian authorities ever repudiate these views?
faq.answer: Explicitly. Pope John Paul II's 1995 Letter to Women said of the church's share in women's marginalization, "for this I am truly sorry," and his 1988 letter Mulieris Dignitatem grounded women's equal dignity in creation. Modern Catholic teaching abandoned the Aristotelian biology behind Aquinas's language; Protestant egalitarians argue equality of role is the Bible's own position; and even complementarian statements such as the Danvers Statement affirm women's equal worth while rejecting any language of defectiveness. Churches across traditions have also repudiated counsel that women endure abuse, as in the US Catholic bishops' statement When I Call for Help.

faq.question: Does Aquinas's "misbegotten" claim come from the Bible or from Aristotle?
faq.answer: From Aristotle. The phrase "the female is a misbegotten male" enters Summa Theologiae I q.92 a.1 as a quotation from Aristotle's Generation of Animals, whose embryology held that male seed aims to reproduce a male and that a female birth reflects a defect in that process. Aquinas accepted this as the science of his day at the individual level while insisting that woman is fully intended by God in nature as a whole and fully bears the image of God in its principal sense. Critics such as Joe Early (CBE International) argue the damage of the framework outlived the science; defenders note the framework collapsed with the science — both agree the biology is obsolete.

furtherReading.title: Women in the Early Church (Message of the Fathers of the Church 13)
furtherReading.author: Elizabeth A. Clark
furtherReading.note: A standard scholarly sourcebook of patristic texts on women — including Tertullian and Jerome — with historical commentary by a leading historian of early Christianity.

furtherReading.title: al-Muhaddithat: The Women Scholars in Islam
furtherReading.author: Mohammad Akram Nadwi
furtherReading.note: The introductory volume to a multi-volume biographical dictionary documenting thousands of women hadith scholars across Islamic history.

furtherReading.title: The Making of Biblical Womanhood: How the Subjugation of Women Became Gospel Truth
furtherReading.author: Beth Allison Barr
furtherReading.note: A Baptist historian's argument that Christian gender hierarchy reflects cultural history rather than the Bible's own teaching — a representative voice of the modern Christian internal debate.

furtherReading.title: Thomas Aquinas's 'Misbegotten' Concept of Women (Priscilla Papers 38.4, CBE International, 2024)
furtherReading.author: Joe Early
furtherReading.note: The modern Christian critique of Summa Theologiae I q.92 cited in this article, analyzing the Aristotelian biology behind Aquinas's language and its later influence.
